paragraphs.body: Oysters, a weird seemingly weird, unimportant, irrelevant mollusk in the water, to basically anyone who knows what they are. However, if you look at them at a deeper level, you realize just how extraordinary and vital these little sea creatures are. Throughout the recent years oyster harvesting has become more and more common, making overharvesting and the dwindling population of oysters a growing concern for scientist, organizations, and communities on the Chesapeake Bay. One of the main organizations working to help restore, conserve, and promote the vitality and quantity of oysters is NOAA Fisheries (Fisheries). NOAA Fisheries has been working tirelessly to restore the lost populations of oysters in the different tributaries of the Chesapeake Bay.

(“How We Save the Bay”) You may be reading this, rather you are a current scientist working on oyster rehabilitation, a member of the Chesapeake Bay community, an oyster farmer, or just someone curious about oysters in the Mid-Atlantic region of the United States. Wondering, “why should I listen to this random scientist trying to help the oysters,” if I am being honest, I would be thinking the same thing. I am not here to try and take your oysters away, to destroy the harvesting that you live off of, I promise, I understand it. Being from the Bay area, I have been surrounded by the effects of the Bay and rivers surrounding us, now I may have not been from an oyster harvesting family, nor really a fishing for economic benefits, I see how important it is for other people. I can also see how trusting and believing these scientists who come into uncharted territory, appearing to try to take away your “lifeline” is daunting and hard to trust. However, I am here to tell you, members and fishers of the Bay, that they are just trying to help. And to the scientists here trying to restore the oyster population, I am here trying to tell you that this is very needed and very crucial for the health of the rivers, Bay, and watershed, that you have to remember, these are peoples jobs, income, and lifestyle that you are dealing with.

annotations.context_left: remember, these are peoples jobs, income, and lifestyle that you are dealing with.

paragraphs.body: People of the Guinea Neck Community in Virginia (Ammons) In the article, Oyster Reef Restoration in the Chesapeake Bay: We’re Making Significant Progress, NOAA Chesapeake Bay Office informs us on the importance oysters have on the Chesapeake Bays water health, ecosystem, and the economy surrounding the Bay. They illustrated their point by developing several important ideas throughout the article. First, NOAA explains the effects oysters have on the ecosystem of the Chesapeake Bay, providing habitats for species, their ability to filter and clean the water, and their importance in the economy of the Chesapeake Bay communities. Oysters have major effects on the ecosystem in the water from improving the water quality, providing habitat to the animals that live in the reefs, stabilizing coastal sediments, and even help fight against climate change. Next, the article states the different ways how the oyster population declining from over harvesting and disease is starting to have a negative impact on the water quality of the Chesapeake Bay. Finally, NOAA proposes the different ways they are trying to conserve and restore the oyster population in the Chesapeake Bay, by opening oyster hatcheries, wild harvest sectors, aquaculture, and reef restoration, all resulting in NOAA reaching eight of their ten targets in reef restoration by 2023.

paragraphs.body: Growing up in Williamsburg, Virginia, a small historic colonial town that is around a ten-minute walk from the beaches of the James River. Oysters have been a large part of my life from the day I was born. Now, I have never eaten an oyster, been oyster harvesting, or even really felt an oyster, they still have major impacts on everything around me. I have always had the privilege of witnessing the vibrant community of the Chesapeake Bay, a majority of it stimming from the shared love of the water. Having the ability to say oysters have been a large part of your life is not a common one I know, however, being from the watershed of the Chesapeake Bay, it is all but uncommon. I can guarantee that walking around downtowns of the historic triangle you will see countless boats harvesting, science research facilities, restaurants that serve fresh oysters, all with the beauty of the water encapsulating your surroundings. Even though they were not a big economic part of mine or my friends’ lives, they were definitely a big part culturally, even if it goes unbeknownst to most.

annotations.text: Growing up in Williamsburg, Virginia, a small historic colonial town that is around a ten-minute walk from the beaches of the James River. Oysters have been a large part of my life from the day I was born.

paragraphs.body: Local oyster restaurant in Williamsburg, VA (“Tripadvisor”) According to Bay Bulletin, “Historically, oysters are also a pillar in the Bay’s seafood industry and traditional fishing culture. The tension between their ecological and economic importance has led to conflict, especially in Maryland, where watermen and environmentalists have feuded over regulating harvests, creating harvest-free sanctuaries and spending large sums of state and federal funds to restore reef habitat in some of them” (Bay Bulletin). The essence of Bulletin’s argument is highlighting the importance of oysters from different perspectives. For locals, oysters and oyster harvesting is important for a economic standpoint. While for scientists, it is important to limit the harvesting, restoring their population in hopes of improving the Bays health. This is such a split issue, one where you may ask, “why listen to this writer who does not seem to understand?” In this I say, I get it, I feel for both, I understand what vibrancy and importance oysters bring to the Bay community, I have lived it, I love it. But, I also see how important it is for restoration, limiting harvesting, all for the health of the Bay, out of the love for the Bay. So please, scientist, harvesters, community, love the Bay, help make it healthier, help spread the life that it has provided to us for our lives.

(“How We Save the Bay”) Citations Ammons, Susan. “New Tour: The Guinea Heritage Driving Tour.” Gloucester County, VA, Gloucester County, VA, 4 Aug. 2023, gloucesterva.gov/tourism/blog/new-tour-the-guinea-heritage-driving-tour. Bay Bulletin. “Md. Considers Easing Oyster Harvest Limits | Chesapeake Bay Magazine.” Chesapeake Bay Magazine, 18 June 2021, www.chesapeakebaymagazine.com/md-considers-easing-oyster-harvest-limits/. “How We Save the Bay.” Www.cbf.org, www.cbf.org/how-we-save-the-bay/index.html. NOAA Fisheries. “Oyster Reef Restoration in the Chesapeake Bay: We’re Making Significant Progress.” NOAA, 2024, www.fisheries.noaa.gov/chesapeake-bay/oyster-reef-restoration-chesapeake-bay-were-making-significant-progress. “Tripadvisor.” Tripadvisor, 2024, www.tripadvisor.com/Restaurants-g58313-zfd10922- Williamsburg_Virginia-Oysters.html.
